request.question: Tomcat in Love is a novel by Tomcat in Love is a novel by best known for a collection of linked short stories called what?

title: Noon: 22nd Century
A. L. Barker
Joseph Polansky
The Heart of Princess Osra
Tomcat in Love

sentences: Noon: 22nd Century (Russian: Полдень.
XXII век, "Polden'.
Dvadcat' vtoroy vek") is a 1961 science fiction book by Arkady and Boris Strugatsky, expanded in 1962 and further in 1967, translated into English in 1978.
It is sometimes considered an episodic novel, collection of linked short stories or a fix-up as some parts had been published previously as independent short stories.
It relates several stories of the 22nd century, while providing the background "feel" for the style of life which gave birth to the Noon Universe.
Audrey Lilian Barker FRSL (13 April 1918 – 21 February 2002) was an English novelist and short story writer.
She was born in St Pauls Cray, Kent and brought up in Beckenham.
During her lifetime, she published ten collections of short stories and eleven novels, one of which - "John Brown's Body" - was shortlisted for the Booker Prize in 1970.
She was also the winner of the inaugural Somerset Maugham Prize in 1947, with her collection of short stories called "Innocents".
Joseph Polansky is an astrologer who has been practicing since 1974.
His articles have been published in a number of astrological magazines.
His books include 'Sun Sign Success', 'Pendulum Power' (which he co-wrote with Greg Nielsen), "A Technique for Meditation", "The Moving Universe" and "A Spiritual Look at the 12 Signs".
His "Your Personal Horoscope" series has been published yearly since 1994 and has been a best seller.
He has published a novel "The Magic Stone" and a collection of short stories called "The Graduate".
He has three more volumes of stories, all of a spiritual nature, yet to be published.
He also publishes a quarterly esoteric review "Diamond Fire" and a personal newsletter "The Astrologer’s Notebook".
The Heart of Princess Osra is part of Anthony Hope's trilogy of novels set in the fictional country of Ruritania and which spawned the genre of Ruritanian romance.

This collection of linked short stories is a prequel: it was written immediately after the success of "The Prisoner of Zenda" and was published in 1896, but is set in the 1730s, well over a century before the events of "Zenda" and its sequel, "Rupert of Hentzau".

The stories deal with the love life of Princess Osra, younger sister of Rudolf III, the shared ancestor of Rudolf Rassendyll, the English gentleman who acts as political decoy in "The Prisoner of Zenda", and Rudolph V of the House of Elphberg, the absolute monarch of that Germanic kingdom.
Tomcat in Love is a novel by Tim O'Brien, about the misadventures of a womanizing linguistics Professor, Thomas H. Chippering, originally published in hardcover by Broadway Books, in 1998.
Chippering is obsessive about proper use of the English language, and employs many examples of wordplay.
Written in the first person, the tale unfolds with extensive use of flashback (what Chippering would insist be called "analepsis") and foreshadowing.
However, he is an incorrigible liar, an indefatigable flirt, and completely self-centered.
"Tomcat in Love" is more picaresque than "Bildungsroman".
Palo Alto is a collection of linked short stories by American actor and writer James Franco.
The collection was published in 2010 by Scribner's.
The stories are about teenagers and their experiments with vices and their struggles with their families.
The book is named after his home town of Palo Alto, California, and is dedicated to many of the writers he worked with at Brooklyn College.

Inspired by some of Franco's own teenage memories, and memories written and submitted by high school students at Palo Alto Senior High School, the stories describe life in Palo Alto as experienced by a series of teenagers who spend most of their time indulging in driving drunk, using drugs and taking part in unplanned acts of violence.
Each passage is told by a young narrator.
William Timothy "Tim" O'Brien (born October 1, 1946) is an American novelist.
He best known for his "The Things They Carried" (1990), a collection of linked short stories.
These semi-autobiographical stories were inspired by O'Brien's experiences in the Vietnam War.
In 2010 the "New York Times" described O'Brien's book as a Vietnam classic.
In addition, he is known for his war novel, "Going After Cacciato" (1978), also about wartime Vietnam, and later novels about postwar lives of veterans.
Larabi's Ox: Stories of Morocco by Tony Ardizzone is a collection of linked short stories.

Published in 1992 by the small press Milkweed Editions, the collection is the Winner of the Milkweed National Fiction Prize, the Friends of Literature's Chicago Foundation Award for Fiction, the Pushcart Prize, and a National Endowment for the Arts Fellowship in Fiction
Mai Al-Nakib is author of "The Hidden Light of Objects", a collection of linked short stories published by Bloomsbury in 2014.
The collection won the Edinburgh International Book Festival's 2014 First Book Award, the first collection of short stories to win.
Her short stories have appeared in "Ninth Letter""," "The First Line", and the anthology "Novel of the World""."
Two of her stories received honorable mention in "Glimmer Train" competitions.
"The Hidden Light of Objects" is her first collection of short stories.
"The Necklace of Princess Fiorimonde" is a classic fairy tale written by Mary de Morgan (1850–1907) in her collection of short stories called "The Necklace of Princess Fiorimonde and Other Stories."
This collection of fairy tales originally published in 1880.
Mary de Morgan helped to make the Victorian era prominent in literature.
In her short stories, she is able to use "mystery, pathos, and comedy" to create entertaining and imaginative literature for all to enjoy.
In addition, de Morgan uses both female and male protagonists in her writing exhibiting her belief in equality among the sexes.
In many of her works, Mary de Morgan uses elements from the folk tales of medieval England.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Tomcat in Love is a novel by Tomcat in Love is a novel by best known for a collection of linked short stories called what?
hop1_docs: Tomcat in Love | Tomcat in Love is a novel by Tim O'Brien, about the misadventures of a womanizing linguistics Professor, Thomas H. Chippering, originally published in hardcover by Broadway Books, in 1998.  Chippering is obsessive about proper use of the English language, and employs many examples of wordplay.  Written in the first person, the tale unfolds with extensive use of flashback (what Chippering would insist be called "analepsis") and foreshadowing.  However, he is an incorrigible liar, an indefatigable flirt, and completely self-centered.  "Tomcat in Love" is more picaresque than "Bildungsroman".

K. L. Cook | Kenny "K. L." Cook is an American writer from Texas.  He is the author of "Last Call" (2004), a collection of linked stories spanning thirty-two years in the life of a West Texas family, the novel, "The Girl From Charnelle" (2006), and the short story collection, "Love Songs for the Quarantined" (2011).

Joyce Carol Oates | Joyce Carol Oates (born June 16, 1938) is an American writer.  Oates published her first book in 1963 and has since published over 40 novels, as well as a number of plays and novellas, and many volumes of short stories, poetry, and nonfiction.  She has won many awards for her writing, including the National Book Award, for her novel "them" (1969), two O. Henry Awards, and the National Humanities Medal.  Her novels "Black Water" (1992), "What I Lived For" (1994), "Blonde" (2000), and short story collections "The Wheel of Love" (1970) and "Lovely, Dark, Deep: Stories" (2014) were each finalists for the Pulitzer Prize.

What Is This Thing Called Love? (short story) | "What Is This Thing Called Love?"  is a science fiction short story by Isaac Asimov.  The story was requested by Cele Goldsmith Lalli, editor of "Amazing Stories", as a satire of a story in "Playboy" magazine called "Girls for the Slime God", which proposed — not too seriously — that all science fiction stories were concerned with aliens and sex.  Asimov set out to write a story telling how a sex-interested alien and humans might "really" interact.  The story appeared in the March 1961 issue of "Amazing" as "Playboy and the Slime God", but when Asimov included it in his 1969 collection "Nightfall and Other Stories" he retitled it "What Is This Thing Called Love?"  Goldsmith Lalli rewrote the story's last three paragraphs, a change Asimov regarded as a great improvement, and which he kept.

The Price of Love (short story collection) | The Price of Love (2009) is the second collection of short stories by Peter Robinson; stories previously published in crime anthologies and magazines.  They include an Inspector Banks novella and three Inspector Banks short stories.  Canadian detective fiction writer Peter Robinson is the writer of the multi award-winning Inspector Banks series of novels.  Eleven stories, subtitled "Eleven ways to pay with your life".

Naomi Benaron | Naomi Benaron is an American author.  With her novel "Running the Rift" she won the 2010 Bellwether Prize for fiction.  This is awarded to a first novel which contributed to a literature of social engagement.  Her collection of short stories "Love Letter from a Fat Man", won the 2006 G.S. Sharat Chandra Prize for Fiction.  Both the novel and the short story collection dealt with the Rwandan genocide.  Ms Benaron has also published poetry and short stories in many journals and anthologies.

Christopher Barzak | Christopher Barzak (born July 21, 1975) is an American author.  He has published many short stories, beginning with "A Mad Tea Party" in "Lady Churchill's Rosebud Wristlet" in 1999.  In 2007 he published his debut novel, "One for Sorrow", which won the 2008 Crawford Award, and was a nominee for the 2008 Great Lakes Book Award as well as Logo TV's NewNowNext Awards.  His second novel, "The Love We Share Without Knowing", was a 2008 James Tiptree Jr.  Award finalist and a 2009 Nebula Awards finalist for Best Novel.  His first full-length short story collection, "Before and Afterlives", was the recipient of the Shirley Jackson Award for Best Single-Author Collection in 2013.
hop1_titles: What Is This Thing Called Love? (short story)
The Price of Love (short story collection)
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Tim O'Brien — passage 1 ("Tomcat in Love is a novel by Tim O'Brien")
  - No passage here names a linked short-story collection by Tim O'Brien.
- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Question wording is garbled but seems to ask: "Tim O'Brien is best known for a collection of linked short stories called what?" That collection name is missing from the passages.
- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The title of the linked short-story collection for which Tim O'Brien is best known (i.e., the specific collection name).
- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Tim O'Brien (author) — full Wikipedia/biography page.
  2. The Things They Carried — page or entry for Tim O'Brien's linked short-story collection.
  3. Query: "Tim O'Brien best known for linked short stories" or "Tim O'Brien linked short story collection title".
response.hop2_query: Tim O'Brien linked short story collection title
hop2_docs: Tim O'Brien (author) | William Timothy "Tim" O'Brien (born October 1, 1946) is an American novelist.  He best known for his "The Things They Carried" (1990), a collection of linked short stories.  These semi-autobiographical stories were inspired by O'Brien's experiences in the Vietnam War.  In 2010 the "New York Times" described O'Brien's book as a Vietnam classic.  In addition, he is known for his war novel, "Going After Cacciato" (1978), also about wartime Vietnam, and later novels about postwar lives of veterans.

The Things They Carried | The Things They Carried (1990) is a collection of linked short stories by American novelist Tim O'Brien, about a platoon of American soldiers fighting on the ground in the Vietnam War.  His third book about the war, it is based upon his experiences as a soldier in the 23rd Infantry Division.

Some of Tim's Stories | Some Of Tim’s Stories is a novel written by S.E Hinton, author of the award-winning novel "The Outsiders".  "Some of Tim’s Stories" is a collection of 14 intertwined, short stories that explores the lives of two cousins-Mike and Terry.  The title character, Tim, is a bartender and is also the author of these stories.

Ambulanse | Ambulance (Original title: "Ambulanse") is a collection of short stories by the Norwegian author Johan Harstad, published in 2002.  The collection contains eleven short stories, all connected to each other.  The main character in one of the stories shows up as an 'extra' in the next, and several stories are told from different angles.  The overall link between the short stories is an ambulance driving through an unnamed city.
Saints and Sinners (short story collection) | Saints and Sinners is a short story collection by Edna O'Brien.  Faber and Faber published it in 2011.

Blood and Water (short story collection) | Blood and Water is a 1993 short story collection of Australian author Tim Winton's drawn from the previous two short story collections, "Scission and Other Stories" and "Minimum Of Two", as well as some previously uncollected stories.
The Turning (short story collection) | The Turning is a collection of short stories by multi-award winning Australian author Tim Winton published in April 2005.
response.summary_2: The Things They Carried. Tim O'Brien is best known for The Things They Carried (1990), a collection of linked short stories about a platoon of American soldiers in the Vietnam War.
